locked_facts: Kinetic energy KE = ½mω²(A² − x²) = ½k(A² − x²) — maximum at mean position (x = 0), zero at extremes (x = ±A)
Potential energy PE = ½mω²x² = ½kx² — zero at mean position, maximum at extremes
Total mechanical energy E = ½mω²A² = ½kA² — constant throughout the motion, independent of x
KE and PE are each periodic with frequency 2ω (twice the oscillation frequency) — they exchange twice per cycle
At x = ±A/√2, kinetic energy equals potential energy (KE = PE = E/2)
Average KE over one full cycle = Average PE over one full cycle = E/2
truth_statements: The total mechanical energy in SHM is conserved and proportional to the square of the amplitude
Kinetic and potential energies continuously interchange but their sum remains constant
Both KE and PE vary with a frequency that is twice the frequency of the oscillation itself
At the mean position, all energy is kinetic; at the extreme positions, all energy is potential
The energy of SHM does not depend on mass and spring constant independently — it depends on kA² or equivalently mω²A²

aha_moment.what_to_show: A stacked energy bar (KE in blue, PE in red) alongside the oscillating particle. As the particle moves, KE and PE bars exchange height, but the total bar height NEVER changes. A horizontal line marks E_total = constant across the entire motion.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: The total energy line is absolutely flat — KE rises as PE falls and vice versa. At x = 0, the bar is entirely blue (all KE). At x = ±A, entirely red (all PE). The total never wavers.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students who think energy is lost during oscillation see that total energy is exactly conserved — it merely transforms between kinetic and potential forms

simulation_states.label: Setup
simulation_states.description: Left panel: a spring-mass system at rest at x = A (fully displaced). Right panel: empty KE-PE-Total energy graph with x-axis from −A to +A. The particle is at the extreme — all energy is potential. PE bar = max, KE bar = 0.
simulation_states.emphasis: At the extreme position, the particle is momentarily at rest — all energy is stored as potential energy in the spring

simulation_states.label: Buildup
simulation_states.description: Left panel: mass oscillates. Right panel: KE (blue parabola opening downward) and PE (red parabola opening upward) are plotted as functions of x. The current position is shown as a moving dot on both curves. Total energy horizontal line drawn.
simulation_states.emphasis: KE is a downward parabola peaking at x = 0. PE is an upward parabola peaking at x = ±A. Their sum is the flat total energy line.

simulation_states.label: Aha
simulation_states.description: The particle oscillates while stacked energy bars (KE blue, PE red) animate in real time. The total bar height is constant. A bold horizontal line at E_total stays perfectly flat. Text: 'Energy transforms, never disappears'.
simulation_states.emphasis: The total energy line is absolutely constant. KE + PE = ½kA² at every instant. Energy is conserved — it just changes form between kinetic and potential.

simulation_states.label: Explore
simulation_states.description: Interactive: adjust amplitude A and spring constant k. Both KE and PE curves rescale. Total energy value updates (E = ½kA²). Doubling amplitude quadruples total energy. A marker at x = A/√2 shows the crossover point where KE = PE.
simulation_states.emphasis: Doubling amplitude quadruples energy (E ∝ A²). At x = A/√2, the KE and PE curves cross — this is the equipartition point.

common_misconceptions.belief: Energy is lost during each oscillation because the particle slows down at the extremes
trigger_phrases: oscillation mein energy kam hoti hai
extremes pe slow hota hai toh energy loss
particle ruk jaata hai toh energy kahan gayi
common_misconceptions.correction: NCERT Ch. 13 states total mechanical energy E = ½kA² remains constant throughout SHM. When the particle slows at the extremes, its kinetic energy converts entirely to potential energy — no energy is lost. The particle speeds up again as PE converts back to KE.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Stacked KE-PE bars with constant total height — when KE drops to zero at extremes, PE rises to fill the full bar exactly

common_misconceptions.belief: Doubling the amplitude doubles the total energy of the oscillation
trigger_phrases: A do guna toh E bhi do guna
common_misconceptions.correction: Total energy E = ½kA² is proportional to the SQUARE of amplitude. Doubling the amplitude quadruples the energy (2A → 4 × ½kA²). This is because both the maximum displacement and the maximum velocity increase.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show energy value updating as amplitude slider changes — doubling A causes E to jump by 4×, not 2×

legend.meaning: Current position of particle on energy curves
